weather: Mostly sunny today, high 67-68°F. Light N-NE winds 5-9 mph. Zero rain chance. Evening turns partly cloudy, low 48-50°F, calm to light N-NW breeze. Pleasant spring day—great for walks with Piper.

local_news.findings: Edmonds City Council approved key contracts and dropped potential tree litigation during its April 7 business meeting. The council dismissed a counterpetition in the Redelfs matter, where a 33-inch-diameter landmark tree was removed without a permit, reducing a $102,245 fine to $20,000. The council also approved a $415,116 contract with Jacobs Engineering for gasification system commissioning at the wastewater treatment plant, reclassified a human resources position, and approved a $419,000 sewer pipe rehabilitation project at 27 locations. Mayor Mike Rosen proclaimed April 9 as Local News Day, recognizing local news organizations' contributions to the community.

The South County Fire Board of Commissioners is considering redistricting proposals to incorporate Edmonds' 42,000+ residents into the Regional Fire Authority following last year's annexation. Two plans have been presented: Plan 1 consolidates almost all of Edmonds into a single district, while Plan 2 splits the city between two districts to preserve current commissioners' districts. The board is seeking public input before finalizing a plan, with public hearings planned. Edmonds has been represented by a non-voting liaison since annexation in June 2025.

Edmonds approved a $415,000 contract with Jacobs Engineering to operate the gasification system at its wastewater treatment plant, marking a potential breakthrough after three years of technical challenges. The system, which turns human waste into dried solids and destroys PFAS chemicals, was the first of its kind in the country when implemented. The city has struggled with equipment failures, smoke plumes, and clogging since initial attempts to launch in April 2023, but remains hopeful the system will work as intended moving forward.

The Washington State Court of Appeals reopened a case involving Edmonds' tree regulations, potentially reviving litigation over the city's tree mandate requirements. The case involves Nathan Rimmer, who sued the city in 2023 after being required to replace a dogwood tree as part of a building permit. While the court had previously dismissed the case as moot after Rimmer sold the property, his attorney has filed a motion for reconsideration, arguing the city should still pay damages for unconstitutional tree replacement requirements.

intellectual_journals.findings: Donald MacKenzie’s essay “AI’s Scale” dissects the cultural imperative of scale in contemporary artificial-intelligence development. He begins with Meta’s new data-centre project “Hyperion” in Louisiana—whose footprint, superimposed on Manhattan, would blanket the island from river to river—arguing that the drive toward ever-larger models is not merely technical but ideological. Tracing the history of neural networks, MacKenzie shows that early sceptics (including Marvin Minsky) dismissed them in favour of symbolic rule-based systems; only when data sets and compute reached gargantuan proporti [TRUNCATED]

A sweeping investigation by ProPublica and FRONTLINE reveals that more than 300 U.S. citizens were arrested during recent immigration sweeps on accusations—often flimsy or fabricated—of assaulting or interfering with federal agents. In over a third of the cases examined, charges collapsed once video evidence contradicted officer statements; prosecutors quietly dismissed cases or lost at trial. The report spotlights Alejandro Orellana, a Marine veteran arrested in an East L.A. raid televised by Fox News, whose supposed “conspiracy” evaporated within weeks. Legal scholars warn that even baseless [TRUNCATED]

Maria Popova’s long essay on Frances “Fanny” Wright recovers the forgotten 19th-century Scottish-American radical who fused scientific empiricism with utopian social experiment. Orphaned young and educated in Glasgow’s university circles, Wright crossed the Atlantic in 1818, scandalising polite society by advocating free love, birth control and interracial communitarianism. Her Nashoba commune in Tennessee—an audacious attempt to prove that emancipated slaves could thrive alongside white settlers—failed amid malaria and financial ruin, yet her lectures drew thousands and influenced Walt Whitma [TRUNCATED]

Francine Prose reviews two new novels—Lee Clay Johnson’s “Bloodline” and Carl Hiaasen’s “Fever Beach”—that confront America’s epidemic of conspiratorial grievance through dark satire. Johnson’s protagonist, Winston Alcorn, believes himself descended from Confederate General John Hunt Morgan and stages a gruesome insurance scam (sawing off his own hand) to reclaim ancestral land in Tennessee. Hiaasen’s Florida tale likewise pits ecological outrage against heritage myth-making, as a half-indigenous veteran hunts polluters who desecrate Seminole burial grounds. Prose argues that both writers depl [TRUNCATED]

triadic_ontology.findings: Two new works advance the triadic-ontology program.  Christopher Isabelle’s “Triadic Relational Ontology: From Primordial Separation to Relational Field” (PhilArchive, 2026) argues that dyadic logic collapses without an actively-maintained third term—a relational field that must be sustained through tension between poles or intelligibility itself disappears.  Building on his earlier Primordial Separation Ontology, Isabelle positions language as a compression of this triadic structure and sketches non-linear dynamics for how the field is re-constituted moment-to-moment.  Meanwhile the Karl-Alber series “Studies on Triadic Ontology and Trinitarian Philosophy” has released two 2026 volumes: “Restoring Reason” (ed. Enders & Falque) and “Kinder der Trinität” by emerging scholar Mátyás Szalay, both extending relational models of divine personhood and exploring quantum-style superposition as a metaphor for trinitarian perichoresis.  Together these papers push the conversation from static meta [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: President Trump's February 2026 directive to release government files on UFOs and extraterrestrial life has triggered unprecedented momentum in UAP disclosure. Rep. Anna Paulina Luna, chairing the House Oversight Task Force on Declassification, promises an imminent press conference revealing "materials of nonhuman origin" observed in classified briefings. The Pentagon's All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO) is coordinating with the National Archives to digitize and release UAP records under NDAA mandates, though delays persist on 46 requested military videos. A classified March 25 SCIF briefing has intensified scrutiny following the mysterious disappearance of retired Air Force General Neil McCasland—former commander of Wright-Patterson's research lab—whose vanishing coincided with Trump's disclosure timeline. Christopher Mellon notes this marks the first presidential directive with genuine authority to compel cross-agency transparency, while whistleblowers caution that reverse-e [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: Ellen Burstyn’s Inner Library
newyorker.dek: Kris Kristofferson told her he was a poet when they co-starred in “Alice Doesn’t Live Here Anymore.” Her new book tells the story of her life in poetry, Michael Schulman writes.
newyorker.text:
The actress Ellen Burstyn has what she calls an “inner library” of poetry that she has memorized in the course of her long life. “I’m ninety-three now, so I probably have less in my head than I used to when it comes to poems,” she said the other day. “But some have been there for years. Decades.” Burstyn was in her Upper West Side apartment, which was festooned with books, plants, crystals, orbs, and statues of Shiva and Buddha and Guanyin (“the goddess of compassion,” she noted). Meditative music piped in through the sound system. “I was getting a massage, so it’s left over,” she explained.
She wore green slacks, a green turtleneck, and a green cardigan (“I do like green”), with chunky metallic necklaces. In her writing room, lined with shelves of Mary Oliver and Rumi—her Oscar, for “Alice Doesn’t Live Here Anymore,” was across the hall—she showed off the desk where she wrote her new book, “Poetry Says It Better,” in which she revisits her life story through verses she has loved. “I like what metaphors set off in your mind,” she said. “I like what poetry plays with.”
She settled into a wicker chair in the living room, beneath a wooden mermaid that she got in Mexico. Her cavapoo, Kerri, sat regally on a divan. The first poem that spoke to Burstyn, she recalled, was William Ernest Henley’s “Invictus,” which she read in high school, in Detroit. “ ‘I am the master of my fate, I am the captain of my soul’—that awakened an understanding in me that life was not always going to be the way it was.” At school, she was head cheerleader, but her home life was arduous. “My mother married a man who hated her two children,” she said. “I was being punished a lot physically.” She wrote her own poem and gave it to a boy in her class. “He said, ‘Just because your name is Edna’—which was my original name—‘doesn’t mean you have to copy her style.’ I said, ‘Who?’ ” That’s how she discovered Edna St. Vincent Millay. “It was like she was my voice,” she said, and rattled off Millay’s sonnet “Love Is Not All.”
At nineteen, Burstyn married her first husband, a poet and car salesman (“In Detroit, everybody’s involved with cars”), who introduced her to Baudelaire. “When I read Baudelaire now, I go, Boy, he’s really dark!” she said. By twenty-four, she was modelling in New York and decided to become an actress. “I went around saying, ‘I’m going to do a Broadway play this fall. Do you know how I can get an audition?’ ” It worked: she made her Broadway début in 1957, in the comedy “Fair Game.” Her determination put her in mind of the poem “For Longing,” by John O’Donohue: “May you have the courage to listen to the voice of desire.”
Some years later, in Hollywood, she was acting in the Debbie Reynolds flick “Goodbye Charlie.” “I said, ‘This is it. This is the big time. Next I’ll be playing Debbie Reynolds’s part!’ And this voice in my head said, I don’t want it,” she recalled. Through Shelley Winters, she met the acting teacher Lee Strasberg and studied with him. “I did a scene for him, and he said, ‘I don’t know, dear. You either laugh or you cry. If you would laugh and cry at the same time, then we’d see something.’ ” She found herself doing just that in her breakout film, “The Last Picture Show.” This reminded her of what the poet Wisława Szymborska wrote about the soul: “Joy and sorrow / aren’t two different feelings for it.”
Burstyn starred in “Alice” in 1974, alongside Kris Kristofferson, who insisted that he couldn’t act or sing. “What is it you do?” she remembered asking him. “I am a poet,” he answered. In her book, she included a sampling of his lyrics. (“He’s a walkin’ contradiction, partly truth and partly fiction.”) Did any poems remind her of starring in “The Exorcist”? Not quite, she said, but the film does open with the Muslim call to prayer. In the seventies, inspired by an etching of a chanting muezzin in her copy of “The Rubáiyát of Omar Khayyám,” she began travelling the Middle East. In Iran, she visited the Tomb of Hafez. “People come rea [TRUNCATED]

literary_pick.title: The Line of Beauty
literary_pick.author: Alan Hollinghurst
literary_pick.summary: Winner of the 2004 Man Booker Prize, Alan Hollinghurst's The Line of Beauty is an intricate portrait of class, politics, and sexuality in Margaret Thatcher's 1980s Britain. The novel follows Nick Guest, a young gay man who becomes entangled in the world of the wealthy Feddens family, navigating the excesses and moral complexities of the era. Through Nick's increasingly less innocent perspective, Hollinghurst explores themes of privilege, power, and the AIDS crisis, crafting what critics have hailed as a defining novel of its decade and a landmark in LGBTQ+ literature.
